title: Government approval was the foundation
subtitle: Two years of sub-30% Yoon approval pre-determined the verdict
thesis: The 22nd election was decided less by campaign moments and more by the cumulative effect of persistent sub-30% Yoon administration approval. The structural mid-term verdict was visible in vote-intention polling from January 2024 onward; no late campaign event reversed the broad direction, though PPP narrowed the margin from a 12-point deficit in early January to 6.58 points on election eve.

sections.heading: The approval baseline (2022–2024)
paragraphs: Yoon Suk Yeol entered office in May 2022 with relatively modest approval and never recovered above 40% for any sustained period. By August 2022, ratings had dropped from the low 30s to 24% following backlash over education-minister Park Soon-ae's school-reform plans. A September 2022 controversy over a hot-mic profanity at the UN Global Fund pulled the rating to 27.7% — down 3.7 percentage points in three weeks.

Through 2023 and into 2024, Korean Wikipedia describes approval as stuck in the 20% range with continuously low levels. This was the foundational condition under which the 22nd election was contested: not a brief slump but a durable mid-term verdict already crystallized.

sections.heading: Vote intention tracked the same trajectory
paragraphs: Polling for the National Assembly election captured a consistent DPK lead throughout the campaign. In January 2024 (Korea Research / MBC), DPK led PPP 45–33 — a 12-point margin. By the end of March (Research View, March 29–31), the gap was 11.4 points (49.1 vs 37.7). In the final week (Korea Research / MBC, April 2–3), it stood at 9.2 points (50.1 vs 40.9). The final pre-election poll on April 10 had DPK 52.34, PPP 45.76 — a 6.58-point lead, narrower but still decisive.

The actual outcome (DPK 175 seats / PPP 108 nationally; 37–11 in Seoul) tracked the late polling within roughly one percentage point. A pure-mood reading would predict approximately this distribution; campaign moments may have closed the margin somewhat but did not change the direction.

sections.heading: What the gap-closing tells us
paragraphs: The 12pp → 6.58pp narrowing over three months is meaningful but bounded. It suggests campaign dynamics moved votes at the margin — possibly through PPP-stronghold mobilization in the Gangnam-Seocho-Songpa cluster, plus high-profile candidate races (Na Kyung-won in Dongjak 을, Kwon Young-se in Yongsan). It does not suggest the underlying mid-term verdict was reversible.

A counterfactual where Yoon approval had recovered to roughly 40% during the 2023–2024 stretch would likely have produced a meaningfully closer contest. The 2024 election was, in effect, contested on terrain set in 2022–2023.

sections.heading: What we cannot yet say
paragraphs: Without access to BigKinds-grade media-attention data or weekly Korea Gallup approval breakdowns through the 2022–2024 period, we cannot quantify how much each named controversy (Itaewon, Chae Sang-byeol, Kim Keon-hee handbag, Busan Expo, doctor strike, green onion) moved the overall approval level versus serving as cumulative reinforcement of an already-low baseline.

Post-election survey data (KEPS / 한국선거학회) would be the cleanest test: did voters who turned out specifically cite Yoon governance as their primary motivation, or did their preferences track demographic priors with national mood as a secondary factor? Both readings are consistent with the polling, but they imply different things for prediction.

key_data_points.label: Yoon approval (2023–2024 baseline)
key_data_points.value: Persistently in the 20% range per Korean Wikipedia
